risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to the treatment or control group in a 1 : 1 ratio and stratified on BMI category (overweight: BMI 25–29.99 versus obese: 30–39.99), age (≥40 years), and current antidepressant medication use (n = 7) ... Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation method. The explicit use of computer-generated random numbers, with 1:1 allocation and stratification, is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

_evidence.method_quote: Participants were randomized to the treatment or control group in a 1 : 1 ratio and stratified on BMI category (overweight: BMI 25–29.99 versus obese: 30–39.99), age (≥40 years), and current antidepressant medication use (n = 7) because these factors are known to influence weight and may impact change in abdominal fat over time. Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition.
_evidence.method_summary: Computer-generated random numbers were used to assign participants 1:1, with stratification by BMI category, age, and antidepressant use.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: This indicates central allocation by the site statistician, with assignment revealed only after baseline assessments were completed and then relayed to staff; the recruiter could not foresee the next assignment before enrollment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." "participants were unblinded to the hypotheses of the study about stress and abdominal fat, which could affect behavior in both groups." Comment: Participants explicitly knew the study hypotheses, and the authors state this could affect behavior. Because the intervention included multiple subjective/self-report outcomes (e.g., perceived stress, anxiety, eating behavior, mindfulness) that could plausibly be influenced by participants' and personnel's knowledge of allocation, the dominant risk for performance bias is high. Personnel blinding was also incomplete: "Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires ... but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments."

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses.” “Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments.” “participants were unblinded to the hypotheses of the study about stress and abdominal fat, which could affect behavior in both groups.”
_evidence.blinding_method: waitlist-controlled; both groups participated in a 2-hour nutrition and exercise information session midway through the intervention, in which mindfulness was not discussed

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments." Comment: The study used mixed outcome assessors: blinded nurses for objective measures, but non-blinded research assistants for computerized questionnaires. Because the questionnaires were patient-reported/subjective outcomes (mindfulness, stress, anxiety, eating behavior), knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of any unblinded subjective outcome makes the overall domain high risk, even though objective measures were assessed blind.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments.

outcomes.label: computerized questionnaires (mindfulness, stress, anxiety, eating behavior)
saliva sampling procedure instructions / home saliva collection

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Five treatment and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis" and "Primary analyses used independent samples t-tests ... Assuming participants lost to followup did not change over time, missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values. Treatment efficacy analyses were also performed by including treatment participants who attended at least 4/10 classes and excluding one control participant who received liposuction." Comment: The analysed outcome had observed post-randomization attrition of 5/24 vs 2/23 (14.9% total) with an 8.5 percentage-point imbalance, plus a treatment-efficacy/as-treated analysis that excluded participants based on adherence and excluded a control participant after randomization. The use of simple baseline-value imputation and a post-hoc efficacy analysis materially deviates from strict randomised analysis, so attrition could bias the result.

per_arm_flow.reasons: "Five treatment ... participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis"; also "Four treatment participants did not receive the minimum treatment dose"

"... and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis"; "One control participant received liposuction and was included in the intention-to-treat analyses but was excluded from treatment efficacy and secondary analyses involving any biological outcomes."

_evidence.missing_data_method: ITT; missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values; treatment efficacy analysis; as-treated
_evidence.notes: Primary analysis used intention-to-treat with postintervention missing data imputed using preintervention values. Treatment efficacy analyses excluded one control participant who received liposuction and included only treatment participants who attended at least 4/10 classes.
